skill.name: omero-integration
skill.description: Guidance for using OMERO with Python to connect to microscopy servers, retrieve images, manage metadata, process pixels, handle ROIs, and store analysis results.
skill.summary: Build Python workflows for OMERO microscopy data access, analysis, annotations, ROIs, tables, and batch scripts.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static external-command and weak-cryptography alerts were reviewed as false positives from Markdown fences and non-cryptographic example text. The real risk is moderate because the skill teaches OMERO network access, credentials, server-side scripts, file transfer, destructive deletes, and admin workflows. No evidence of prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious exfiltration was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Destructive OMERO Operations in Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The documentation shows deleteObjects examples for projects, datasets, images, ROIs, annotations, and table files. This is legitimate OMERO administration guidance, but copied code could remove research data without additional confirmation or backups.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct deleteObjects examples are present across multiple references. The risk is behavioral misuse, not confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Administrative and Cross-User Access Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The advanced reference includes administrator checks, listing administrators and users, setting object ownership, and substitute-user connections. These examples are legitimate for OMERO admins but could expose user metadata or enable overbroad actions if used without policy controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly perform administrative enumeration and substitute-user workflows. The examples require legitimate OMERO privileges.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Admin Credential Placeholder
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: A complete admin example sets ADMIN_USER to root and ADMIN_PASS to password. The value appears to be a placeholder, but it normalizes hardcoded privileged credentials in generated guidance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded admin username and password are directly visible. It is probably illustrative, so the issue is insecure guidance rather than credential leakage.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized Local Download Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: File download examples join a local directory with filenames supplied by OMERO file metadata and then write the result. If a filename contains path separators, generated code based on this pattern could overwrite unintended local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write path is constructed from server-provided filenames without a visible basename or path containment check. Actual exploitability depends on OMERO filename constraints.

medium_findings.title: Server-Side Script Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill teaches OMERO.scripts that run on the OMERO server and can process batches of images. This is core functionality for the skill, but generated scripts need review because they execute in a privileged server-side environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly define server-side OMERO scripts. The capability is legitimate, but the execution context increases operational risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The many Ruby or shell backtick alerts align with Markdown code fences and inline documentation. No evidence found that the skill itself invokes shell commands or Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences or static documentation examples, not executable skill code. Broad review found no shell execution API use.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Representative flagged lines are descriptions, MIME types, image creation labels, or table text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, custom cryptography, or insecure cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations do not contain cryptographic functions or algorithms. The signal appears caused by substring or heuristic matching.

low_findings.title: Credential Environment Access Is Legitimate but Sensitive
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The connection guide reads OMERO credentials from environment variables and configuration. This is a safer pattern than hardcoding, but users must avoid logging secrets and must protect local configuration files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples intentionally access credentials for OMERO authentication and include a best-practice warning. This is sensitive but expected for the skill domain.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The network URL alerts in SKILL.md point to OMERO documentation and community support pages. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or trigger runtime network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static documentation references. They are not code paths and do not exfiltrate data.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection and Obfuscation Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Review found no suspicious instructions such as override commands, pre-approval claims, or requests to skip security analysis. The obfuscation heuristic did not match malicious content in the reviewed Markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of injection terms found only ordinary administrator wording in OMERO documentation. No malicious instruction text was present.

dangerous_patterns.title: deleteObjects Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: OMERO deleteObjects examples can remove projects, datasets, images, ROIs, annotations, links, and files when run with sufficient permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous API is shown directly in the examples. Its impact depends on caller permissions and selected object IDs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Administrator Substitute-User Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The advanced guide shows administrator login and substitute-user access, which can broaden access beyond ordinary user context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow is explicit and uses OMERO administrative features. It is legitimate but requires strict operational controls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Writes from Server Metadata
dangerous_patterns.description: Download examples write files using names obtained from OMERO metadata without visible path sanitization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and write are visible. The likelihood depends on whether OMERO accepts path separator characters in file names.

dangerous_patterns.title: Server-Side OMERO Script Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reference provides templates for code that runs on an OMERO server and processes user-selected data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution model is described directly in the reference. The examples are not malicious, but server execution raises review requirements.

content.value_statement: Microscopy teams need reliable examples for OMERO data access, metadata, and analysis automation. This skill provides focused Python guidance for connecting to OMERO, processing images, managing ROIs, and storing results.
actual_capabilities: Shows Python connection patterns for OMERO servers using BlitzGateway.
Explains how to retrieve projects, datasets, images, plates, wells, and metadata.
Provides examples for reading pixel data, thumbnails, rendering settings, and derived images.
Covers creating, querying, and analyzing ROIs with common shape types.
Documents annotations, file attachments, OMERO tables, and server-side batch scripts.
Includes advanced guidance for permissions, group context, deletion, and administrative operations.
limitations: Requires access to an OMERO server and valid credentials.
Does not provide a tested application or reusable Python package.
Examples must be reviewed before use on production or shared datasets.
Administrative and delete examples can change or remove data if copied without safeguards.

use_cases.title: Explore microscopy datasets
use_cases.description: List OMERO projects, datasets, images, plates, wells, annotations, owners, and acquisition metadata.

use_cases.title: Automate image analysis
use_cases.description: Read pixel planes into Python, calculate measurements, create derived images, and save analysis outputs.

use_cases.title: Build batch processing scripts
use_cases.description: Create OMERO.scripts that process selected images, report progress, and return tables or file attachments.

prompt_templates.title: Connect to OMERO
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show a safe Python connection pattern for OMERO using environment variables and secure connections.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve Dataset Images
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to write guidance for listing images in an OMERO dataset and printing each image name and ID.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze ROIs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a workflow that reads ROIs from OMERO images and stores intensity measurements in a table.

prompt_templates.title: Design Batch Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an OMERO.scripts workflow for batch maximum intensity projections with validation and cleanup.

output_examples.input: Ask for a safe connection pattern for OMERO.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow that uses environment variables, secure transport, context managers, and clear connection cleanup.

output_examples.input: Ask how to measure ROI intensity across a dataset.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan covering image retrieval, ROI traversal, pixel access, measurement calculation, and table storage.

output_examples.input: Ask how to attach analysis results to a dataset.
output_examples.output: Guidance for creating file annotations, choosing namespaces, linking outputs, and documenting result metadata.

best_practices: Use secure connections and never hardcode real OMERO credentials.
Validate object IDs, permissions, and group context before reading, writing, or deleting data.
Test scripts on small datasets before using them on production microscopy repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not copy administrative examples into production without approval and audit logging.
Do not write downloaded files using server-provided names without checking the final path.
Do not run delete operations without explicit confirmation and a recovery plan.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps generate Python guidance for OMERO microscopy data access, image analysis, annotations, ROIs, tables, and scripts.

faq.question: Does it connect to OMERO by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides examples and guidance. Users still need OMERO access, credentials, and a Python environment.

faq.question: Can it process high-content screening data?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for screens, plates, wells, well samples, images, and batch analysis patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe for production data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review generated code, protect credentials, and test any write, delete, or admin operation first.

faq.question: Does it cover metadata and annotations?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers tags, map annotations, file annotations, comments, namespaces, and annotation queries.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
